text.title: AI-Generated Test Validation Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every ai-generated-test-validation artefact must obey. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Tests MUST NOT assert the implementation by copying it. A test that re-states the same arithmetic as the function under test is rejected.
rule.rationale: Mirror tests pass even when the function returns the wrong answer; they detect typos, not bugs.

rule.statement: Every test MUST contain at least one assertion that checks a behavioural invariant (output, side-effect, raised exception). No-assertion tests are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without an assertion the test cannot fail; coverage rises but defect-detection does not.

rule.statement: Tests MUST NOT mock the system under test. Mocking the unit being verified converts the test into an assertion about the mock.
rule.rationale: Mocking the SUT proves the mock returns what you told it to; it proves nothing about the production code.

rule.statement: Mutation-survival rate (mutants killed / mutants generated) on AI-generated tests MUST be >= 0.70 for the file to ship. Below that, tests are reverted.

rationale.source: Petrović et al. — On the efficacy of mutation analysis

rule.rationale: Below 70% kill rate, the test suite gives the wrong impression that the code is covered; refactors break silently.

rule.statement: Every test MUST be shown to fail against at least one deliberately-broken version of the production code (the mutation step does this automatically).
rule.rationale: Assertions that never fail under any mutation are vacuous; the test suite reports clean even when the code is broken.
